title: Bridge to Bigger Builds: Systems for Scaling from $1M to $5M Projects
summary: Erin presented her 'Bridge to Bigger Builds' framework, walking through nine to ten core systems she developed to scale from smaller projects to $5M+ builds, including budgeting, scheduling, communication, cash flow, project management, team building, and client experience. Participants then discussed real challenges including managing difficult high-end clients, finding and vetting trades, staffing structure, CRM platforms, and bridging the gap between pre-construction and construction phases. The session emphasized that implementing these systems places builders in the top 3-5% of the industry.

teachings.explanation: Scaling from smaller to larger projects requires deliberately transitioning from casual, owner-managed operations to structured, delegatable systems across budgeting, scheduling, communication, cash flow, project management, trades selection, and client experience. Each system must be formalized before taking on higher-value clients.
teachings.quote: A $1 million client is a very different client than a $5 million client. A $5 million client is very different than a $20 million client.

teachings.explanation: Every line in a budget must be grounded in fact. If a number is an allowance or estimate, it must be clearly labeled as such with a note. Only confirmed quotes and actual selections can be committed to as hard numbers. This prevents surprises and builds client trust.
teachings.quote: Every budget is built with facts and the facts are, this is an allowance. The fact is we don't have the details yet. And that's a note. This is what we think for now, but we're going to revisit this later. Or this is a real quote with a real roof.

teachings.concept: Micro Build Schedules for Client Communication
teachings.explanation: Rather than overwhelming clients with full Gantt charts, communicating four-week rolling schedules gives clients enough visibility to flag changes before it's too late, such as noticing a countertop edge detail before fabrication is complete. Internal teams use longer-range schedules while clients receive the digestible four-week view.
teachings.quote: We communicate a micro build schedule. And it could be a two-week schedule or a four-week schedule. We've landed on four-week schedules because it's enough for a client to look at and see and say, oh wow, the countertops are supposed to go in a month from now.

teachings.concept: Construction Completion Aligned with Client Payments
teachings.explanation: Billing should always be proportional to actual construction progress. Charging 50% when only 25% of work is done creates financial misalignment and risk. Keeping billing in sync with progress protects both the builder's integrity and the client's trust.
teachings.quote: If you're 25% done on a project, 25% of the bill should be paid. We don't charge the client 50% if we're only 25% there. And it doesn't matter what contract you have. That should always align.

teachings.explanation: Consistent, non-negotiable reporting cadences reduce client anxiety and prevent unnecessary escalations. When clients know exactly when and how they will receive updates, they stop calling with worry. Missing a scheduled report without notice erodes trust even when project work is on track.
teachings.quote: Do not invite any misalignment, any mistrust. We do what we say we're going to do. Send a report Monday, every Monday. If you can't send it on a Monday, that client needs to be told.

teachings.concept: Project Manager as Owner of the Project
teachings.explanation: Project managers must act as the accountable leader of their project, not passive coordinators. They are responsible for driving scheduling, trades, reporting, and execution. Allowing them to drift into a follower role leads to delays and missed deliverables.
teachings.quote: You are in charge of this project. Lots of different teammates, be a great teammate, but your job is to get this thing done. It will not get done without you. So get to it.

teachings.concept: Selling Standards and Experience, Not Price
teachings.explanation: At higher project values, clients are hiring the builder's systems, standards, and reliability, not just the owner personally. Competing on price is a losing strategy at the high end. Clients will pay for an experience that makes them feel secure, and the builder's job is to help them articulate what that experience is worth to them.
teachings.quote: People are hiring our standards. They're not hiring the owner of the company, right? Because if the owner of the company gets hit by a bus, what's their risk? A lot of risk if you don't have standards and processes and systems in play.

teachings.explanation: Implementing structured systems across budgeting, scheduling, communication, and cash flow places a builder in the top 3-5% of the industry because the vast majority of builders do not practice these disciplines. This competitive differentiation justifies premium pricing and attracts better clients.
teachings.quote: 95% of the industry doesn't do any of this. So, literally, just by checking the boxes here, you're now in the top 5%, maybe top 3% of how you can serve clients.

teachings.explanation: Finding the right trade for a specific project goal requires using existing trusted relationships, such as asking material reps, architects, and other trades for referrals, then walking completed work in person. Asking one trusted trade who they love working with is one of the most effective ways to build a reliable trade network.
teachings.quote: I talked to the trim carpenter. Hey, which painters do you love working with that love working with you? And then he would give me two and I would go look at their work.

teachings.concept: Honest Schedule Padding Based on Known Trade Patterns
teachings.explanation: When a trade gives an optimistic timeline, the project manager's responsibility is to adjust that timeline in the schedule based on known patterns, not to accept the trade's word at face value. This produces accurate schedules that protect the client and the project from false expectations.
teachings.quote: Hey, so-and-so, I know you told me three weeks. I'm going to say it's nine weeks. Does that sound reasonable? Like stop them in their tracks. Don't ever think they're telling you the truth. But that's our responsibility to plug that into the calendar.

actionable_advice: Fill out the Bridge to Bigger Builds scorecard honestly to identify where your current systems stand and where gaps exist.
Send invoices to trades on a fixed day each week and pay approved work on a fixed day, so trades know reliability is guaranteed when they perform correctly.
Send weekly client reports on the same day every week without exception; if you must miss the day, call the client in advance to notify them.
Build four-week rolling micro schedules to share with clients so they can flag changes before fabrication or installation locks things in.
When a trade gives you an estimated timeline, document a longer realistic timeline in the schedule based on your actual experience with that trade.
When a trade is underperforming, have a direct conversation immediately rather than waiting; ask them to explain their planning process and work collaboratively to solve the problem.
When vetting trades for a specialized need, call a trusted material rep or supplier and ask them to set up walk-throughs of work by their top recommended contractors.
Ask trusted trades on your team who their favorite complementary trades are, then go inspect that work before hiring them.
Pull the assigned project manager into the final stage of pre-construction pricing so they absorb key project details before construction begins.
Dedicate a daily learning block, even just one hour in the morning or during exercise, to studying a problem or topic relevant to your business.
client_challenges: Rebecca described high-end clients in San Pedro Garza Garcia, Mexico who forget agreements they signed and become aggressive when reminded of their commitments, making it extremely difficult to enforce process and timelines.
Rebecca's client was withholding weekly payments to trades out of dissatisfaction, causing cascading delays across all subcontractors on a large project.
Rebecca's client fired multiple specialist contractors mid-project, including a landscape firm that had traveled from Florida, causing significant disruption.
Rebecca described trades in Mexico consistently delivering work weeks later than their stated timelines, and clients expecting that money can accelerate delivery regardless of actual constraints.
Lauren asked whether there is a recommended CRM platform for managing both the sales funnel and client communication during construction, or whether spreadsheets are sufficient.
Lauren asked whether there is a recommended staff-to-revenue or staff-to-project ratio to ensure the right team capacity is in place as the business scales.
David asked how to navigate a situation where a trade crew is performing at a passable but not ideal level mid-project, and how to pivot without destroying the relationship.

Kami and Jamie raised the challenge of disconnects between the pre-construction pricing phase and the construction execution phase, with the project manager not having full context on decisions made earlier.
notable_quotes: On cash flow reality: 'Plenty of times I've looked at cash flow and I'm like, am I going to make it? Truly, am I going to make it? And you can either pick yes or no. If it's yes, then it's these are the things that I actively need to do right now.'
On what building really is: 'Building literally is telling the truth. Planning ahead, creating stability, be reliable, consistently show up.'

On hiring conservatively: 'I have to like almost be dead before I hire someone. Like it's literally like, I play pretty conservative. I call it hired a pain. Like when the pain gets some, like yeah, no, you're right.'

On the no-caulk baseboard challenge: 'I love your request. I love it. Who's the trim carpenter? And he said, good question. Very good question. I said, if this is the goal and you're putting this on me, you need to tell me who you've done this successfully with.'
On selling an experience not a product: 'You could run all the comps you want. You can justify and logic your way to building a home. But what I'm hearing is, you want an experience.'
On leadership clarity in the field: 'A lack of leadership leaves people in a place where they have to rip out a bunch of work they've done, lose money. And that's on the leader.'
